query: has AZD9668 been tested in clinical trials

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Data on the secretion of deucravacitinib in human milk, its effects on lactogenesis, and its effects on breastfed infants are not available. During animal studies, deucravacitinib was found in the milk of rats. While prescribing deucravacitinib, the health benefits of breastfeeding infants and the clinical need for therapy must be weighed against the potential adverse effects on the breastfed child. [3] Pediatric patients: The safety and effectiveness of deucravacitinib have not been established in the pediatric patient population.

retrieved_docs.title: Desmoid Tumor -- Treatment / Management -- Nirogacestat
retrieved_docs.content: Nirogacestat (150 mg twice a day) is the first gamma-secretase inhibitor granted full USFDA approval for patients with DT whose tumors are progressing. Nirogacestat was studied in a phase III, double-blinded randomized control trial against a placebo. The drug was approved based on the significant benefit of the progression-free survival (hazard ratio for disease progression or death, 0.29; 95% confidence interval, 0.15-0.55; P <0.001), response rate (41% versus 8%, P <0.001) and a significant reduction in pain, symptoms burden, physical and functional role. Diarrhea, nausea, fatigue, and rash were amongst the significant grade 3 or higher adverse events. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Mastocytosis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous drugs are undergoing clinical trial investigations. Mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) and phosphatidylinositol 3 kinase (PI3K) target downstream KIT signaling. A study evaluating the effect of multi-kinase inhibitor DCC-2618 on proliferation indicated a promising result that the agent can reduce the proliferation and survival of neoplastic mast cells. Therefore it can play a role in advanced systemic mastocytosis. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Keratoconjunctivitis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Ongoing Clinical Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Novel ρ kinase inhibitors: Trials investigating ρ kinase inhibitors are focused on their ability to promote corneal endothelial healing and reduce inflammation. If successful, these inhibitors could provide an innovative approach to managing refractory keratoconjunctivitis. [101]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Selonsertib is a first-in-class small-molecule (apoptosis signal-regulating kinase 1 [ASK1]) inhibitor developed to treat diseases with a high burden of oxidative stress, including PAH. [99] The randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2 ARROW trial included 151 study participants. [100] All were aged 18 to 75 years and had a diagnosis of PAH. From December 2014 to November 2015, patients were stratified by PAH etiology and background therapy, and underwent random assignment to selonsertib 2 mg, selonsertib 6 mg, selonsertib 18 mg, or placebo administered once daily via tablet. The primary outcome was a change in PVR from baseline to 24 weeks, as measured by RHC. Mean change in PVR was 6 dynes/cm in the placebo group, 35 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 2-mg group ( P = .21), 28 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 6-mg group ( P = .27) and –21 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 18-mg group ( P = .60). [100] Additionally, the investigators reported no improvement in secondary outcomes such as exercise capacity, symptom burden, or worsening clinical events.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Glomerular filtration rate: During the placebo-controlled 16-week period, for patients with moderate impairment of renal function (eGFR 30 to 59 mL/min) at baseline, reduction in GFR reductions were observed in 4 patients (1.6 per 100 patient-years) who were on deucravacitinib treatment, and 1 patient (0.8 per 100 patient-years) on placebo therapy. Two of the patients treated with deucravacitinib had worsening baseline proteinuria. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Deucravacitinib has consistently demonstrated a safety profile. [3] The safety of deucravacitinib was assessed in 2 placebo-controlled and active-controlled (PSO-1 and PSO-2) clinical trials and an open-label-extension (OLE) trial in which patients could enroll after completing the PSO-1 or PSO-2 trials. A total of 1519 patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis received deucravacitinib 6 mg once daily. Of these, 1141 patients were exposed to deucravacitinib for at least 12 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Soft Tissue Clear Cell Sarcoma -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The unique translocation and resulting gene fusion, EWSR1-ATF1 , characteristic of CCSST, offers a target for new therapies. Studies are ongoing to investigate the effectiveness of targeted therapies, including tyrosine kinase inhibitors, mesenchymal-epithelial transition (MET) inhibitors, and immunotherapies. [33] Results of trials investigating crizotinib, a MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor, and tivantinib, a selective MET inhibitor, showed only partial response in less than 9% of patients. [42] [43] Another MET inhibitor, ganitumab (AMG 337), was ineffective in a trial of 8 patients with advanced or metastatic clear cell sarcoma ( https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT03132155 ). Partial responses have also been seen in patients receiving the kinase inhibitor sunitinib. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: In phase II clinical trials, the experimental drug testing takes place within a larger group of participants (typically 100 to 300) to determine its efficacy and to further evaluate its safety. Phase II trials usually last for months and involve participants with the target disease or condition. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In 2020, following a clinical trial, the FDA also granted emergency use authorization (EUA) for the use of baricitinib in combination with remdesivir to treat COVID-19 in hospitalized patients who need supplemental oxygen, invasive mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). [3] Subsequently, the EUA was revised to authorize baricitinib as a standalone treatment for adult patients. However, for pediatric patients aged 2 to 17, baricitinib continues to be under EUA status. [4] Clinical trials have shown promising results for the use of baricitinib in the treatment of alopecia areata. [2] Following successful outcomes in clinical trials BRAVE-AA1 and BRAVE-AA2, baricitinib has received FDA approval for treating severe alopecia areata in adults. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Adrenocortical Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Recently, drugs targeting IGF-II seemed to be very promising, as IGF-II is the most-up regulated gene in ACC (80% as described before). Preclinical studies antagonizing this pathway with pharmacological agents resulted in inhibition of growth in vitro and in vivo. This inhibition was more potent than that observed with the use of mitotane alone in decreasing xenograft growth, and the combination of IGF inhibition with mitotane resulted in greater antiproliferative effects than those observed with the use of single-agent treatment. These data establish the role of targeted disruption of IGF-1R signaling to obtain a therapeutic advantage when used with mitotane therapy or possibly other chemotherapeutics in ACC patients. [106] Recently, a phase 2 study used an IMCA12 (cixutumumab), a fully humanized IGF-IR antibody showed a lack of efficacy in a cohort of 19 patients. [107] Also, results of a huge phase 3 trial “GALACCTIC” with a highly specific IGF-IR inhibitor linstinib (OSI-906)in a cohort of 138 metastatic ACC have been recently published: the progression-free and overall survival did not differ between the linstinib and placebo groups. [108] However, in another study, 26 heavily pretreated ACC patients received cixutumumab with temsirolimus, an inhibitor of mammalian targets of IGF-1R signaling (mTOR inhibitor)weekly with restaging at eight weeks. Of these 26 patients, 11 (42%) had stable disease for greater than six months. [109] Finally, because of disease heterogeneity, it appears that using one single agent is not sufficient to induce an objective response. Trials with new targeted substances are underway.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Creatine phosphokinase: In the placebo-controlled period of 16 weeks, elevated creatine phosphokinase (CPK) levels, including Grade 4 severity, were observed in 23 patients (9.3 per 100 patient-years) receiving deucravacitinib therapy and 5 patients (4.1 per 100 patient-years) who were on placebo treatment. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: During trials, baricitinib was detected in rat milk. Although it is unknown whether baricitinib is present in human milk, breastfeeding mothers are advised against its use. Preferably, an alternative drug should be considered, especially when nursing a newborn or preterm infant. The manufacturer recommends refraining from nursing during therapy and for 4 days after the last dose. [16] Pediatric patients: The FDA has issued the EUA for baricitinib to treat COVID-19 in hospitalized pediatric patients aged 2 to 17 who require supplemental oxygen, ECMO, or mechanical ventilation. [17] Older patients: No specific dose adjustment for baricitinib has been recommended for the older population.

retrieved_docs.title: Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For High-Risk Coronavirus (COVID 19) Patients With Mild To Moderate Disease Presentations (Archived) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Bamlanivimab (LY-CoV555 or LY3819253) is a potent neutralizing monoclonal antibody derived from convalescent plasma obtained from a patient with COVID-19. REGN-COV2 also targets the RBD of the spike protein of SARS-CoV-2 or S protein and has been shown to potently neutralize SARS-CoV-2 and reduce viral replication in non-human primates. [17] [18] Results from the phase 2 trial (BLAZE-1) involving outpatients with a recent diagnosis of mild to moderate COVID-19 who were randomized to receive 1 of 3 doses (700 mg, 2800 mg, or 7000 mg) of bamlanivimab or placebo reported that patients who received bamlanivimab monotherapy did not have a significant decline in viral load compared to placebo. However, in this cohort, bamlanivimab reduced the risk of COVID-19 hospitalization or emergency department (ED) visits on day 29 by 70%. This interim analysis also established the safety profile of bamlanivimab, which was similar to that of the placebo group at all three doses. [19] A preprint study reported that bamlanivimab still binds to the Y501-RBD of SARS-CoV-2 (B.1.1.7, 20I/501Y.V1) in vitro, as efficiently as the previous N501-RBD of the original strain. [19] A separate preprint study by the same authors reported that the B.1.351/501Y.V2 variant and P.1/501Y.V3 variant, which share three similar mutations (K417N, E484K, and N501Y), completely lost binding to bamlanivimab in vitro, causing complete loss of efficacy of bamlanivimab. [20] Bamlanivimab received EUA by FDA in November 2020 for clinical use in non-hospitalized patients with mild to moderate COVID-19 who are at increased risk for developing severe disease and/or hospitalization; however, the company that manufactures this product requested that the FDA revoke the EUA for bamlanivimab alone on April 16th, 2021 in light of the emergence of earlier SARS-CoV-2 variants which demonstrated resistance to bamlanivimab monotherapy. [18] However, the EUA for bamlanivimab has since been reinstated when used in combination with etesevimab, another monoclonal antibody.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: At week 16 in the PSO-1 trial, significantly higher response rates were observed in patients receiving deucravacitinib compared to placebo or apremilast. Therapeutic efficacy improved with treatment longer than 16 weeks and was maintained through week 52. At week 16 in the PSO-2 trial, more than 50% of patients receiving deucravacitinib achieved superior clearing compared to apremilast. Across multiple efficacy endpoints, deucravacitinib consistently demonstrated superiority to apremilast and was well tolerated in patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis. [25] A large proportion of patients treated with deucravacitinib reported a Psoriasis Symptoms and Signs Diary (PSSD) symptoms score of 0 at week 16, indicating relief from itching, pain, stinging, burning, and skin tightness (8% in deucravacitinib group versus 1% in the placebo arm) in both clinical trials. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors Baricitinib is an oral selective inhibitor of Janus kinase (JAK) 1 and JAK 2 currently indicated for moderate to severely active rheumatoid arthritis patients. Baricitinib was considered a potential treatment for COVID-19 based on its inhibitory effect on SARS-CoV-2 endocytosis in vitro and on the intracellular signaling pathway of cytokines that cause the late-onset hyperinflammatory state that results in severe illness. [115] [116] This dual inhibitory effect makes it a promising therapeutic drug against all stages of COVID-19. A multicenter observational, retrospective study of 113 hospitalized patients with COVID-19 pneumonia who received baricitinib combined with lopinavir/ritonavir (baricitinib arm, n=113) or hydroxychloroquine and lopinavir/ritonavir (control arm, n=78) reported significant improvement in clinical symptoms and 2-week mortality rate in the baricitinib arm compared with the control arm. Results from the ACTT-2 trial, a double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluating baricitinib plus remdesivir in hospitalized adult patients with COVID-19, reported that the combination therapy of baricitinib plus remdesivir was superior to remdesivir therapy alone in not only reducing recovery time but also accelerating clinical improvement in hospitalized patients with COVID-19, particularly who were receiving high flow oxygen supplementation or non-invasive ventilation. [117] Baricitinib, in combination with remdesivir, has been approved for clinical use in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 under a EUA issued by the FDA. The efficacy of baricitinib alone or in combination with remdesivir has not been evaluated in the SARS-CoV-2 variants, and there is limited data on the use of baricitinib with dexamethasone. Ruxolitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and 2 indicated for myeloproliferative disorders, polycythemia vera, and steroid-resistant GVHD. Similar to baricitinib, it has been hypothesized to have an inhibitory effect on cytokines' intracellular signaling pathway, making it a potential treatment against COVID-19. Results from a small prospective multicenter randomized controlled phase 2 trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of ruxolitinib reported no statistical difference from the standard of care. However, most patients demonstrated significant chest CT improvement and faster recovery from lymphopenia. [118] A large randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled multicenter trial (NCT04362137) is ongoing to assess ruxolitinib's efficacy and safety in patients with severe COVID-19. Tofacitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and JAK3 that is indicated for moderate to severe RA, psoriatic arthritis, and moderate to severe ulcerative colitis. Given its inhibitory effect on the inflammatory cascade, it was hypothesized that its use could ameliorate the viral inflammation-mediated lung injury in patients with severe COVID-19. Results from a small randomized controlled trial that evaluated the efficacy involving 289 patients who were randomized to receive tofacitinib or placebo showed that tofacitinib led to a lower risk of respiratory failure or death (PMID:34133856).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1290
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and gilteritinib are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group designated “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” indicating that each drug belongs to this specific category of protein kinase–targeting agents. This classification reflects their shared mechanism of action as protein kinase inhibitors within the broader ATC system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 966
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen, inclisiran, alirocumab, evolocumab, policosanol, and tiadenol are all grouped within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) class “Other lipid‑modifying agents.” Within this class, mipomersen is noted for its physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, a mechanism that contributes to its lipid‑lowering activity. Both policosanol and tiadenol are chemically linked to fatty alcohols (mastné alkoholy), indicating that they share structural characteristics typical of fatty‑alcohol derivatives. Recognizing that these six agents belong to the same therapeutic category and understanding their distinct mechanisms—such as mipomersen’s impact on protein synthesis and the fatty‑alcohol nature of policosanol and tiadenol—helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑modifying therapies and anticipate specific pharmacologic effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib; they inhibit the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway and are employed in hematologic malignancies, with dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib and imatinib mesylát indicated for lymphoid leukemia and ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, another protein‑kinase inhibitor subgroup, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib and target Bruton's tyrosine‑kinase; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, highlighting BTK inhibition in lymphoid cancers. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class and exemplify kinase‑targeted therapy beyond leukemias. The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors” also contains agents such as cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin have been identified as potential treatments for acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these protein‑kinase inhibitors—targeting BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases—constitute a network of antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs that underpin contemporary therapeutic strategies for lymphoid and myeloid leukemias and other cancers.
